div.style: clear: both; text-align: left; padding-left: 20px; padding-right: 20px;

div.div: An example of assigning a shielding to be a self-attenuating source for U235 and U238. The relative abundance of U235 and U238 will also be fit for.

p: Self-attenuating sources are particularly useful for higher mass-number elements like Tungsten, Thorium, Uranium and Plutonium because these dense materials often provide a significant amount of attenuation before the gammas leave the object, and the radioactive isotopes are distributed over the volume of the object, making point-source approximations very poor.

InterSpec uses a ray-trace algorithm coupled to an adaptive quadrature based numerical integration scheme to take into account the attenuation in the shieldings on the way to the detector, from the entire shieldings volume, to calculate the expected full-energy peak areas.

div.style: clear: both; text-align: left; padding-left: 20px; padding-right: 20px;

div.div:
[Left] how to add a trace source to a shielding.
[Right] Example of how a shielding area looks after adding a trace source to it.

p: You can add as many trace source isotopes to a shielding as you have in the currently selected peaks to use in the fit. However, you can only use each isotope in your problem in a maximum of one shielding as either a self-attenuating source, or trace source.

If you select the trace source to be of type "per m^2 exp", the source term will be exponentially distributed from the surface of the shielding. This option is most often used for soil contamination, using an end-on cylindrical, or a rectangular geometry. When this option is selected you will have to enter a "Relaxation Distance", which is the distance from the surface at which 63% of the contamination is contained within. See the InterSpec tutorials further information.

A current limitation in InterSpec is that detector response function does not have a angular dependent component, and assumes the same efficiency no matter the angle or position of the incoming gamma ray. For volumetric sources that are comparable or larger than the distance to the detector, this can result in some inaccuracies. For surface contamination measurements with the detector 1 meter from the ground, this may lead to inaccuracies of 10% or so, with lower energy gammas generally being effected more.

div.style: clear: both; text-align: left; padding-left: 20px; padding-right: 20px;

div.div:
[Left] Example χ-style chart showing number of statistical sigmas each peak is away from the models nominal value.
[Right] The same data, but presented in the Mult.-style chart option. 1-sigma error bars are shown for each data-point, with some errors being smaller than visible.

p:
This chart can help to identify situations when not enough shielding is present (the data points have a general positive trend), too much shielding is present (data points have a general negative trend), or the effective atomic number of the shielding is to high or too low (the data points are concave upwards or downwards after fitting).
[Left] Example of too much shielding being present, indicated by the clear trend of data points at the different energies.
[Right] Example of shielding being too low of an atomic number, as shown by data points generally being concave downwards.

To help make the judgements described above, InterSpec automatically fits a smooth trend to the χ-chart points and, when warranted, shows a short one-line interpretation just beneath the chart. This appears only after you Perform Model Fit; while you are editing the model by hand (for example changing an atomic number without re-fitting) the dashed trend curve keeps updating, but the text is hidden, because the interpretation is only meaningful once the activity and shielding have actually been optimized.

div.style: clear: both; text-align: left; padding-left: 20px; padding-right: 20px;

div.div: The dashed curve is the fitted trend through the peak residuals, and the italic text below the chart is the automated interpretation (here, a concave-downward trend indicating the modeled effective atomic number is too low). The Mult./χ toggle at the right switches the chart style; the trend curve is only drawn in the χ style.

p: How the trend is fit. Under a correct model each residual is an independent draw from a unit-normal distribution, so a plain (unit-weight) least-squares fit of the residuals versus log(energy) is used. When more than one nuclide is present a single joint fit is performed: a separate offset (intercept) is allowed for each nuclide — since each nuclide's activity is fit independently, its residuals already average to about zero — while the slope and curvature are shared across all nuclides, because every peak sees the same shielding. A quadratic is fit first; if its curvature term is not statistically justified it falls back to a straight line, and if the slope is not justified either, no trend is drawn.

What the shape means. The slope reflects the amount of shielding, and the curvature reflects its effective atomic number:
rising with energy → the model has too little shielding; falling → too much;
concave downward (∩) → the effective atomic number is too low; concave upward (∪) → too high.
Which of these is reported depends on what you let the fit vary. If a shielding thickness or areal density was fit, the amount is already optimal, so a residual shape is attributed to the atomic number. If both the atomic number and areal density of a generic shielding were fit, neither can be blamed and any remaining trend is reported as a general inconsistency (suggesting distance, geometry, source age, or detector response). Self-attenuating and trace sources are not interpreted.

Uncertainties and confidence. Only the statistical uncertainty of each fit peak is used (the same as for the chart itself). Real peak fits are, however, somewhat over-dispersed relative to pure counting statistics, so the uncertainty on the fitted slope and curvature is scaled by the square root of the reduced χ2 of the residuals about the trend. This keeps the significance (t = coefficient / its uncertainty) well-scaled, and importantly it down-weights poorly-converged fits that could otherwise masquerade as a strong trend. The wording of the message reflects the magnitude of t:
possibly (|t| ≥ 1.5) — a soft advisory; expect a fair number of these to be statistical flukes,
may (|t| ≥ 2.5), probably (|t| ≥ 4), strongly (|t| ≥ 6) — progressively firmer.

Can the data even tell? (atomic-number power). Above roughly 250 keV the attenuation of all elements has nearly the same shape versus energy (differing only in overall magnitude, which the fitted areal density absorbs); the atomic-number signal lives almost entirely in the lower-energy peaks. So before making any atomic-number call, InterSpec computes a discrimination power: the curvature significance that a reference-sized atomic-number error (a factor of about 1.5 in effective Z) would produce for your particular set of peaks and shielding. If that power is too low — typically because the shielding has removed the low-energy peaks, or there were no low energy peaks available — the atomic-number direction is not asserted (and a "silent" result is noted in the calculation log as "cannot resolve Z" rather than implying the atomic number is fine). This is common for heavily shielded sources, where the amount of shielding can still be judged but its composition cannot.
Further detail about each peak, including these statistics, is available in the calculation log (the log icon on the tool).

ul.li: Multiple nuclides contribute to peak: Currently in InterSpec a single gamma or x-ray is associated with each peak, however often times for spectra with multiple nuclides, or for nuclides with many gammas, more than one source gamma/x-ray may contribute to the observed peak. Checking this option (the default) allows summing the contributions from all nuclides in the fit, and all gammas/x-rays of each nuclide that may contribute to the peak, so that these multiple contributions can be accounted for in calculating the models expected count for the peak.

Attenuate for air: When checked, attenuation due to air will be included when computing expected peak intensities. The standard "Air" material is used for attenuation calculation, with a length equal to the distance you entered to the center of the shielding, minus the outer depth of the shielding in the direction of the detector (e.g., the distance from detector to outside of shielding).
You may want to unselect this option if when you created your detector response function you unselected the "Atten. for air" option, and your measurement you are currently analyzing is at the same distance as your detector characterization. One scenario when you may choose to not include attenuation from air is if you are working with Marinelli beaker geometries, where you created your detector response function using a Marinelli beaker standard and the sample you are analyzing is the same geometry and distance (but also if you include air attenuation in creating the detector response, and in this analysis, things should cancel out anyway - but also if you made the response function in InterSpec, see the "Fixed Geometry" option).

Subtract Background Peaks: If your source peaks correspond to peaks that may also be found in the background, then this option will effectively subtract the background peak amplitudes from the foreground peak amplitudes. To use this feature, first load the background spectrum as a foreground, and fit for all the relevant peaks (you do not need to associate nuclides with them), then load the item of interest as the foreground spectrum and identify peaks associated with nuclides as usual, but also use the File Manager or Spectrum Files tool to load the background file as background to. Once you do this, this option should become available.

Isotopes of same element same age: When fitting for the age of multiple isotopes, that are all of the same element (e.x., U-235 and U-238), then selecting this option will constrain the ages of the nuclides for the same element to all have the same age in the fit.

Correct for decay during meas.: When selected, the contributions of each gamma line are averaged over the measurement dwell interval, with the decay and in-growth of nuclides being accounted for. The resulting fit activities are the activities at the start of the measurement interval. The method used does not account for dead-time effects, or summing effects. The downside to enabling this option is an increase in computation time, and it is not usually necassary for life-times much greater than the measurement interval.

p: Templates are written in the Inja templating language and consume a JSON data model built from the fit results. A complete field reference and authoring guide is shipped at InterSpec_resources/static_text/ShieldSourceFitLog/README.md (also covers the batch peak-fit templates). You can also start from any of the bundled templates — e.g. std_fit_log.tmplt.txt or act_fit.tmplt.html in the same directory — and modify them.

The README is written specifically to be pasted into a web-based AI chatbot (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, etc.) along with a plain-English description of the report you want. The chatbot will produce a complete Inja template that you can save into the act_shield_fit_templates/ directory above and use immediately. If the chatbot has web access, you can also point it at the README's GitHub URL above.

This menu also provides facilities to save/load/export/import model files; note, if you export a model file, you can drag-n-drop this XML file back on to InterSpec to automatically open the Activity/Shielding Fit tool, instead of having to use this menu to import the model.

ul.li:
There are many ways the model may be degenerate given the input data. InterSpec will not be able to detect most of these situations, and you must vigilant about not fitting for degenerative quantities.
An example is that you cant fit for the areal density and atomic number of shielding if you only have two peaks in the spectrum.
Uncertainties given in the fit may not be trustworthy.
The calculation log should be consulted to ensure everything is reasonable.
You must use at least as many peaks in the fit as quantities you want to fit.
All answers must be sanity checked for correctness.
